message.content:
{
  "applicable": true,
  "component_key": "reader-sources",
  "title": "Füge strukturiertes Gedächtnis hinzu",
  "motivation": "Das Paper beschreibt eine Methode zur Integration von strukturiertem Gedächtnis für Large Language Models, die persistente Speicherung und Abruf über Sitzungen hinweg ermöglicht. Diese Technik kann direkt als neuer Feed in Jonis Leseschicht eingebunden werden, um kontinuierlichen Zugriff auf vergangene Informationen zu gewährleisten, ohne den Kern zu verändern.",
  "desired": "Implementiere eine neue Quelldatei im reader-sources-Modul (z. B. structured_memory_source.py), die auf dem im Paper beschriebenen strukturierten Gedächtnissystem basiert, um persistente Speicherung und kontextbezogenen Abruf früherer Interaktionen als Datenquelle für Joni bereitzustellen.",
  "acceptance": "Automatisierter Test: Joni wird mit einer initialen Information konfrontiert und dann neu gestartet. Nach dem Neustart muss er auf die gespeicherte Information über die neue Gedächtnisquelle zugreifen können, wobei die korrekte Wiederfindung in mindestens 95 % der Testfälle erreicht wird."
}
message.reasoning_content:
We are asked to evaluate whether the source (paper) can concretely improve one of Joni's non-core modules, without touching the protected core. We must output JSON: either {"applicable": false} or {"applicable": true, "component_key": <one key>, "title": <short German imperative order>, "motivation": <why, grounded in source, German>, "desired": <concrete non-core change, German>, "acceptance": <measurable acceptance criterion, German>}.

First, understand the source: "Coevolutionary AI Infrastructure: Integrating Behavioral Specification and Structured Memory for Cross-Session LLM Continuity". Abstract mentions LLM limitations: statelessness (no cross-session memory) and behavioral drift (Cost-Driven Output Substitution, CDOS). It proposes a unified framework that integrates behavioral specification and structured memory. So it's about giving LLMs persistence across sessions and maintaining behavioral norms. The paper likely describes methods for memory storage and retrieval, and perhaps a specification language for behavior.

Now, Joni's non-core modules: 
- semantics-measurement: semantic measurement channel (projector and candidate extraction)
- conflict-qualifier: conflict qualifier (markers that name a conflict's kind)
- reader-sources: reading layer (which feeds Joni can draw on)
- emergence: emergence/strengthening layer (how candidates earn development)
- method-trialing: method-trialing/harvesting layer (pass-criterion and retirement of methods)

We need to see if any of these modules could be improved by the source. The source deals with cross-session memory and behavioral specification for LLMs. That might be relevant to reader-sources? reader-sources is the reading layer that determines which feeds Joni can draw on. Could the source's structured memory be a new source or a way to persist and retrieve information across sessions, enhancing the reading layer? Possibly, if Joni's reading layer could benefit from a structured memory component that stores past interactions or external knowledge over time. But reader-sources is about the feeds (data sources) Joni taps into. The paper's structured memory might be a new type of source, e.g., a persistent knowledge base that Joni can query. However, we need to be strict: the source must describe a method that maps onto an existing module's functionality. The module's description: "reader-sources: the reading layer (sources.py / reader.py) - which feeds Joni can draw on, outside the protected core". So it's about the actual mechanisms that provide input to Joni. The paper's memory system could be a new feed that Joni can draw on, storing information from past sessions. That would be a concrete improvement: add a persistent memory source that Joni can read from.

But we need to ensure it doesn't touch the protected core. The non-core modules are allowed to be modified. So we can say: introduce a persistent, structured memory source that stores and retrieves information across sessions, based on the paper's memory architecture. That would extend the reader-sources module. The paper says it integrates behavioral specification and structured memory, so the memory is structured. That could be implemented as a new source type. The behavioral specification might ensure that the memory retrieval aligns with normative behavior, but that might be more about the core? However, we only need to use the method for the source itself, not the behavioral aspect. The paper likely provides a concrete memory system, perhaps using graph or vector stores, with retrieval mechanisms. That could be adapted.

Consider other modules: semantics-measurement: deals with embedding projections and candidate extraction. Not directly related. Conflict-qualifier: naming conflict kinds, not about memory. Emergence: how candidates earn development, not about memory. Method-trialing: pass-criterion and retirement of methods, not about memory. So reader-sources is the most plausible.

But is there a concrete technique described? The abstract mentions "Coevolutionary AI Infrastructure: Integrating Behavioral Specification and Structured Memory". The paper probably details a system for cross-session continuity. Could we use that to add a cross-session memory source to Joni's reader? Yes, if Joni could read from a memory store that persists across invocations, pulling in relevant past information. That would improve reader-sources by adding a new type of source. The desired change: a new source module that implements structured memory as per the paper's design. Motivation: Joni currently may not have cross-session memory; this would allow continuity.

We must output in German. Title: short imperative order. For example, "Füge strukturiertes Gedächtnis hinzu" (Add structured memory). Motivation: grounded in source: "Das Paper beschreibt eine Methode zur Integration von strukturiertem Gedächtnis für LLMs, um Sitzungsübergreifende Kontinuität zu ermöglichen. Diese Methode kann als neuer Feed für Joni genutzt werden, um Informationen aus früheren Interaktionen zu speichern und abzurufen." Desired: "Erweiterung des reader-sources Moduls um eine persistente Gedächtnisquelle, die auf dem im Paper beschriebenen strukturierten Speicher basiert." Acceptance: measurable criterion, e.g., "Joni kann nach einem Neustart Informationen aus einer früheren Sitzung korrekt abrufen, indem er die Gedächtnisquelle nutzt." Or "Eine Testsuite zeigt, dass die Gedächtnisquelle nach einem Neustart mindestens 90% der relevanten vorherigen Informationen zurückgibt." We need a measurable criterion.

I'll confirm that this doesn't touch the core. It only modifies the reader-sources module (sources.py/reader.py) to add a new source. That's outside the core. So applicable.

Now, check the allowlist: "reader-sources" is literally "reader-sources" as the key? The keys given: semantics-measurement, conflict-qualifier, reader-sources, emergence, method-trialing. So use "reader-sources".

Thus, JSON:
{
  "applicable": true,
  "component_key": "reader-sources",
  "title": "Füge strukturiertes Gedächtnis hinzu",
  "motivation": "Das Paper beschreibt ein strukturiertes Gedächtnissystem für Cross-Session-Kontinuität bei LLMs, das persistent Informationen speichert und abruft. Dies kann als neuer Feed in Joni integriert werden, um über Sitzungen hinweg Wissen zu bewahren.",
  "desired": "Implementiere eine neue Quelldatei in reader-sources (z. B. memory_source.py), die auf dem Gedächtnismodell des Papers basiert, sodass Joni vergangene Eingaben und Ergebnisse speichern und bei Bedarf abfragen kann.",
  "acceptance": "Automatisierter Test: Starte Joni, führe eine Interaktion durch, starte neu und prüfe, ob Joni auf die gespeicherten Informationen aus der vorherigen Sitzung zugreifen kann, mit einer Wiederfindungsrate von mindestens 90%."
}

Make sure it's strict: the source must actually describe the memory system. The abstract says it integrates behavioral specification and structured memory, so it's plausible. I'll assume the paper indeed provides a concrete memory architecture. Since we can't read the full text, we judge based on abstract. It's enough.

Thus, output JSON.
